sheets.title: FAANG DSA Gauntlet
sheets.description: This is intentionally overbuilt. No list can guarantee an offer, because real interviews also test communication, speed, debugging, and judgment, but if you can solve this bank cold, explain tradeoffs cleanly, and implement without hints, you are very strongly prepared for FAANG-style DSA rounds. Sources used for calibration: LeetCode Top Interview 150, LeetCode Problemset, NeetCode Roadmap, CSES Problem Set, and cp-algorithms.

sheets.title: FAANG DSA Extreme Mastery Map
sheets.description: This is the heavier replacement for a normal DSA list. It is built to cover the interview surface area, not just popular questions. No document can honestly guarantee a 100% pass rate, because interviews also test communication, speed, debugging, and judgment under pressure. But if you can solve this set cold, explain the invariants, and adapt to variants, you should be ready for almost any FAANG-style DSA interview. Primary calibration sources: LeetCode Problemset, LeetCode Top Interview 150, NeetCode Roadmap, CSES Problem Set, and cp-algorithms.

sheets.title: FAANG DSA Researched Interview Failure Map
sheets.description: This is a correction to the previous DSA sheets. A giant random problem bank is not enough. The real target is the intersection of: 1. What public interview-prep lists repeatedly recommend. 2. What pattern taxonomies say you must recognize. 3. What actually causes candidates to fail under interview pressure. 4. What advanced topics cover the rare but brutal interviewer follow-ups. No prep document can honestly guarantee a 100% pass rate. But this map is built to make your prep much less random.

sheets.title: FAANG System Design and Technical Interview Mastery
sheets.description: This is a practice document for system design, low-level design, and the non-DSA technical material that large tech companies commonly test. No honest prep plan can guarantee a 100% pass rate, but this is designed to make passing highly likely if you can execute the drills under interview conditions. Core references for calibration: System Design Primer Google SRE Book Google SRE Workbook AWS Well-Architected Framework Amazon Builders' Library Designing Data-Intensive Applications Martin Fowler Architecture Guide PostgreSQL Documentation Kafka Documentation Redis Documentation MDN HTTP Overview

items.text: What are the top 3 functional requirements?
What are explicitly out of scope?
Expected QPS, DAU/MAU, data size, latency target?
What would you change at 10x scale?
What metrics prove the system works?
HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 conceptually.
What happens when you type a URL and press enter?
How do you cache personalized content?
How do you handle WebSocket reconnect storms?
When would you choose gRPC over REST?
Why is an index not always good?
How do you choose a shard key?
How do you migrate a billion-row table?
How do you implement exactly-once money transfer semantics?
Where would you place cache in a URL shortener?
How do you avoid cache stampede?
How do you handle hot celebrity profiles?
What happens if Redis goes down?
What data should never be cached?
Why put a queue between upload and processing?
How do you preserve order per user?
How do you handle duplicate events?
How do you replay a day of events?
When is Kafka the wrong choice?
How do you design a distributed lock?
What can go wrong with retries?
How do you handle cross-region writes?
Why is global total ordering expensive?
What alerts would you create for a chat system?
What dashboard would you build for a payment system?
How do you debug high p99 latency?
What is acceptable degradation during an outage?
How do you secure a public API?
How do you prevent credential stuffing?
How do you audit admin actions?
How do you handle GDPR-style deletion?

items.text: Generate short codes: random, base62 counter, Snowflake-style IDs.
Pick patterns only when they help.
Find employees earning more than their manager.
Find users who purchased in 3 consecutive months.
Find conversion rate by experiment group.
Find each user's longest login streak.
Compute account balances from ledger rows.
Find p95 latency per endpoint per hour.
Explain a deadlock and how to prevent it.
What is the difference between mutex and semaphore?
How does an event loop work?
Why can multithreading make a program slower?
What happens when memory is paged out?
How would you implement a thread-safe queue?
How do you debug a race condition?
How do you design a bounded blocking queue?
What happens from browser to server for an HTTPS request?
Why is POST not idempotent by default?
How does DNS caching affect deploys?
How do you handle request retries safely?
What is CORS and why does it exist?
How does TLS prevent man-in-the-middle attacks?
How do load balancers detect unhealthy nodes?
Why do mobile clients need special handling?
Isolate one variable at a time.
Mitigate before root cause if needed.
API p99 latency doubled after deploy.
Cache hit rate dropped from 90% to 40%.
Memory usage grows until process crashes.
A background job processed old data twice.
A new index made writes too slow.

items.text: How did you work with others?
items.tag: For every project on your resume, prepare

items.text: Study networking, databases, caching, queues, and consistency.
Do 4 HLD mocks: URL shortener, chat, feed, video.
Do 4 LLD mocks: parking lot, elevator, rate limiter, file system.
Do 6 HLD mocks: Dropbox, YouTube, Uber, notifications, search, rate limiter.
Deep dive storage and cache tradeoffs.
Study replication, sharding, consensus, queues, exactly-once semantics.
Do Kafka, distributed cache, distributed lock, and analytics pipeline.
Implement 4 designs in code: LRU, rate limiter, in-memory file system, pub/sub.
10 actions per user per day means 10 million writes/day.
1 KB per record times 1 billion records is about 1 TB raw.
Replication and indexes can multiply storage by 3x to 10x.
Cache hit rate changes database load dramatically.
p99 latency usually matters more than average.
What tradeoff did I fail to explain?
What would I say next time?

sheets.title: FAANG System Design And Technical Interview Failure Map
sheets.description: This is the corrected system-design document. The earlier version included useful material, but the source hierarchy was muddy. For interview prep, the core should be interview-specific system design practice. SRE and cloud architecture frameworks are relevant, but only as deep-dive support for reliability, observability, resilience, cost, and operational follow-ups.

items.text: Is the system read-heavy or write-heavy?
What scale should we design for?
Any security, privacy, or compliance requirements?
Idempotency key if mutation can be retried.

groups.title: 1. Identity, Short Links, And Simple Read-Heavy Systems

items.text: delete post from all cached feeds,
payment succeeds but service times out,
What happens if cache is down.
p95 latency per endpoint per hour.
Users who purchased in 3 consecutive months.
Explain why multithreading can be slower.
Explain what happens after entering a URL.
Explain HTTPS handshake at a high level.
Explain DNS TTL impact on migration.
Mitigate if user impact is high.
Deep dive fanout, CDN, object storage, transcoding.
Deep dive indexing, metadata, file sync.
Deep dive partitioning, replication, ordering, replay.
Deep dive geospatial, matching, idempotency, inventory.

sheets.title: FAANG System Design And Technical Concept Repetition Ladders
sheets.description: This document turns system design from "I know the topics" into repeated practice. The goal is to make each concept survive new products, scale changes, failure modes, and interviewer pressure. Use this together with: [FAANG System Design And Technical Interview Failure Map](faang_system_design_researched_interview_failure_map.md) [FAANG System Design and Technical Interview Mastery](faang_system_design_technical_mastery.md)

items.text: User says "like Twitter" but only wants timeline.
User says "real-time" but latency target is 5 seconds.
User says "global" but data residency matters.
User says "payments" but only wallet balance is in scope.
User says "search" but only autocomplete is needed.
Object size is 10 MB instead of 10 KB.
Retention changes from 30 days to 7 years.
p99 latency target changes from 1 second to 100 ms.
Search orders by user and date.
Search messages by conversation and timestamp.
Search products by category and price.
Search reservations by room and time.
Search audit logs by actor and time.
Time-based writes all hit current partition.
Personalized data cannot be CDN cached.
Conflict if same key has different payload.
Exactly once is required by business.
Million jobs due at same minute.
